title: Design major-incident governance from detection to closure
question: How would you design major-incident governance in ServiceNow for a 24x7 operations team?
summary: Covers promotion criteria, bridge handling, communications, and post-incident review.
scenario: The client wants a governed major-incident process with promotion rules, bridge ownership, executive updates, and post-incident follow-up.
exactAnswer: Implement major-incident governance as an escalation operating model on top of incident management: define promotion criteria, assign bridge leadership, automate stakeholder communications, track restoration milestones, and create post-incident review tasks after stabilization.
navigationPath: All > Incident > Administration > Major Incident or incident configurations
All > Now Experience Framework > UI Builder or Workspace config
All > Service Level Management > SLA Definitions
steps: Define the promotion criteria for major incidents, including business criticality and communication triggers.
Configure roles, groups, or workflow ownership for bridge command, communications lead, and technical owner.
Add notifications or communication templates for executive, business, and resolver audiences.
Expose a workspace or form section for timeline, impacted services, and next actions.
Create follow-up tasks for root-cause review and communication closeout after restoration.
Test promotion, update cadence, demotion, and closure scenarios end to end.
validationChecklist: Promotion criteria are consistent and auditable.
Stakeholder communications are timely and not duplicated.
Bridge ownership is visible on the record.
Post-incident review actions are created after restoration.
commonMistakes: Treating every P1 as a major incident automatically.
Sending duplicate updates from multiple notifications and flows.
Not defining who owns communication cadence.

title: Implement a problem-candidate pipeline from repeat incidents
question: How would you implement a repeat-incident detection process that feeds problem management?
summary: Moves from operational symptoms to formal problem investigation.
scenario: The customer wants repeated incidents on the same service or CI to trigger review for problem management instead of waiting for manual detection.
exactAnswer: Implement the pattern by defining threshold logic, building a candidate review queue, and creating a controlled promotion path into formal problem records. The strongest design avoids creating noise while still surfacing genuine repeat-failure patterns.
navigationPath: All > System Definition > Business Rules or Flow Designer
steps: Agree on the threshold dimensions such as CI, service, symptom code, and time window.
Build detection logic using aggregate counting or event-driven review.
Create a candidate queue or flag that problem managers can review before formal conversion.
Store RCA ownership, workaround capture, and known-error actions only on the true problem record.
Track conversion rate and noise rate so thresholds can be tuned.
validationChecklist: Noise is low enough for managers to review.
Repeat incidents are surfaced early.
Formal problem records are created only when warranted.
commonMistakes: Creating problem records automatically for every threshold hit.
Ignoring CI ownership and symptom normalization.

title: Repair SLA behavior after retroactive priority changes
question: How would you fix incorrect SLAs after incidents were updated from P3 to P1 after creation?
summary: Explains historical repair without making things worse.
scenario: A production issue caused incidents to be created at low priority and manually escalated later. The customer wants historical P1 response SLAs corrected for reporting.
exactAnswer: First fix the priority attachment logic going forward, then identify the historical record population, validate which SLA definitions should apply, and run a controlled repair or recalculation approach against only the affected records.
navigationPath: All > Service Level Management > SLA Definitions
All > Service Level Management > Repair tools or related admin path
steps: Validate the corrected priority and SLA definition logic for future records.
Identify the affected historical incidents and confirm which SLA records are wrong.
Remove or recalculate only the incorrect task_sla population, starting in a lower environment.
Run repair on a limited set first and compare expected metrics.
Rebuild impacted reporting only after the corrected task_sla data is confirmed.
validationChecklist: New incidents attach correct SLAs immediately.
Historical corrections match agreed reporting logic.
Repair scope is limited and auditable.
commonMistakes: Running broad repair before validating future-state logic.
Leaving old incorrect task_sla rows alongside new corrected rows.

title: Resolve duplicate CI creation with IRE and source governance
question: How would you stop duplicate servers from being created in CMDB when multiple sources send overlapping data?
summary: Strong CMDB interview scenario around duplicate prevention.
scenario: Discovery, cloud ingestion, and a custom integration are all creating or updating server CIs. The customer sees duplicates and ownership confusion.
exactAnswer: Solve the issue by reviewing identification rules, tightening source payload quality, enforcing IRE for inbound updates, and clarifying reconciliation precedence by source. The best answer mentions cleaning existing duplicates only after future duplicate creation is stopped.
navigationPath: All > Discovery > Schedules or source integrations
All > CMDB Workspace or duplicate review tooling
steps: Inventory the inbound sources and identify which ones are creating versus updating server CIs.
Review identification rule keys to confirm that unique identifiers are present and trustworthy.
Move direct writes to IRE-backed processing where possible.
Define reconciliation precedence so weaker sources do not overwrite trusted data.
Stop new duplicate creation, then plan cleanup and merge strategy for existing duplicates.
validationChecklist: New duplicates stop appearing.
Authoritative fields are no longer overwritten incorrectly.
Existing duplicates are reviewed with merge criteria.
commonMistakes: Cleaning duplicates before fixing the intake pattern.
Using source precedence without trustworthy identifiers.

title: Troubleshoot a MID Server that will not validate
question: How would you troubleshoot a MID Server that remains down or does not validate?
scenario: A new MID Server was installed but remains invalid and discovery schedules do not run through it.
exactAnswer: Troubleshoot in layers: service health on the host, connectivity to the instance, credentials and certificates, ECC queue movement, capability assignment, and whether the target schedule is selecting that MID Server.
steps: Confirm the MID Server service is running on the host and the correct config file values are set.
Check whether the instance can see heartbeats or ECC activity from the MID Server.
Review ECC Queue for errors, backlog, or lack of responses.
Validate capabilities, IP ranges, and schedule selection logic.
Test with a known-good credential and a narrow schedule target.
validationChecklist: MID Server shows validated/up status.
ECC traffic is moving normally.
A test discovery run completes through the intended MID Server.
commonMistakes: Looking only at the MID Server record without checking ECC Queue.
Forgetting capability or schedule selection mismatches.

title: Design a CMDB health dashboard and operating process
question: How would you design a CMDB health dashboard that teams will actually use?
summary: Focuses on governance process, not just visual widgets.
scenario: Leadership wants a dashboard for CMDB quality, but previous dashboards were ignored because they did not drive actions.
exactAnswer: Build the dashboard around accountable actions: show class-level health by owner, trend bad records over time, separate completeness from correctness, and connect each metric to a remediation queue or owner.
navigationPath: All > CMDB > Health or quality administration
steps: Choose metrics that map to action, such as missing owner, stale discovery, or required-field gaps by class.
Assign each metric to a responsible team or owner group.
Trend the metric over time so improvement is visible.
Link the dashboard to record lists or remediation tasks instead of static visualization only.
Review the dashboard regularly in governance meetings and retire metrics nobody uses.
validationChecklist: Every metric has an accountable owner.
Teams can drill into records behind the metric.
Trends are visible over time.
commonMistakes: Showing too many vanity metrics.
Publishing dashboards with no follow-up operating rhythm.

title: Implement a policy-exception lifecycle
question: How would you design a policy-exception workflow that is controlled but usable?
summary: Strong GRC scenario around approvals and expiry.
scenario: The compliance team wants users to request temporary exceptions to policy controls, but they need approval, expiry, and review reminders.
exactAnswer: Design the exception process as a time-bound governed approval: capture justification and risk, require the right approvers, store expiry and review cadence, notify owners before expiry, and convert expired or denied exceptions into remediation follow-up when needed.
navigationPath: All > Flow Designer or workflow tooling
steps: Define required request fields such as policy, justification, affected scope, owner, and requested duration.
Configure approvers based on policy owner and risk/governance ownership.
Store explicit expiry and review dates on the exception record.
Notify owners before expiry and enforce renewal, closure, or remediation actions.
Report on active exceptions by age, owner, and policy area.
validationChecklist: No exception remains open indefinitely without review.
Approvals align to policy ownership.
Renewal and expiry reminders are working.
commonMistakes: Allowing exceptions without an expiry date.
Treating exceptions as a substitute for issue remediation.

title: Route vulnerabilities by CI or service ownership
question: How would you design vulnerability routing when infrastructure ownership is incomplete?
summary: Combines data quality and SecOps workflow design.
scenario: The customer wants vulnerable items assigned automatically, but many CIs have missing or inconsistent support groups.
exactAnswer: Design routing in layers: primary routing from trusted CI or service ownership, fallback routing to platform queues, visibility into unassigned gaps, and a remediation plan to improve CMDB ownership quality over time.
navigationPath: All > Assignment Rules or workflow logic
steps: Identify the preferred ownership field sequence, such as service owner, support group, then platform fallback group.
Implement routing rules with fallback groups for missing ownership.
Create reporting for unresolved ownership gaps so CMDB data quality can improve.
Review routing exceptions regularly with both SecOps and infrastructure owners.
validationChecklist: Most items route automatically.
Fallback groups are not overloaded permanently.
Ownership data quality is visibly improving.
commonMistakes: Assuming ownership data is clean enough before measuring it.
Creating too many narrow groups without accountable teams.

title: Ingest external security alerts into Security Incident Response
question: How would you design an inbound flow from an external security tool into Security Incident Response?
summary: Covers mapping, deduplication, and analyst usability.
scenario: The SOC wants alerts from an external detection tool to create or update security incidents in ServiceNow.
exactAnswer: Design the integration around trusted alert identity, severity mapping, update-versus-create logic, analyst-friendly record structure, and enrichment data that helps triage. The strongest answer includes deduplication and ownership rules.
navigationPath: All > Scripted REST APIs or Integration tooling
All > Import or transform mapping as applicable
steps: Define the inbound payload contract and the unique alert identifier used for deduplication.
Map external severity and classification values to internal security-incident fields.
Decide when new alerts update an existing case versus create a new one.
Include enrichment fields such as source, detection time, host, user, and observable evidence.
Test repeated alerts, reopened alerts, and analyst handoff scenarios.
validationChecklist: Duplicate alerts do not create duplicate cases unnecessarily.
Severity and ownership map correctly.
Analysts can triage from the record without hunting for core context.
commonMistakes: Creating a new case for every repeated alert.
Mapping severity without preserving original source context.

title: Implement a controlled stockroom-transfer process
question: How would you design a stockroom-transfer process for hardware assets?
scenario: The hardware team wants assets to move between stockrooms with accurate inventory and approval history.
exactAnswer: Design the process so transfer requests capture source and destination stockrooms, approved movement reason, custody impact, and final asset/location updates. The best answer mentions auditability and reconciliation against physical moves.
navigationPath: All > Catalog or workflow tooling as needed
steps: Define the transfer request fields including source, destination, reason, requester, and approver.
Ensure destination stockroom validation and authorized movers are in place.
Update the asset record only when the move is approved and confirmed.
Log work notes or transaction history for auditability.
Report on in-transit and completed transfers to catch stranded assets.
validationChecklist: Transfers are approved before inventory changes.
Assets do not disappear between stockrooms.
Transaction history is visible for audit.
commonMistakes: Updating inventory immediately at request creation.
Ignoring custody or location alignment.

title: Design a software-reclamation flow
question: How would you implement software reclamation for underused applications?
summary: Strong SAM scenario around harvesting unused installs.
scenario: The customer wants to reclaim software licenses from users who have the software installed but no longer actively use it.
exactAnswer: Design reclamation around trusted usage or install evidence, user or manager validation, defined removal timelines, and post-reclamation confirmation that license position improved. The strongest answer avoids reclaiming critical software blindly.
navigationPath: All > Flow Designer or task automation
steps: Choose the reclamation population based on usage, install, and business criticality rules.
Create a communication and approval path to confirm removal is acceptable.
Trigger uninstall or task-based removal via endpoint tooling or support teams.
Verify that the install evidence is removed or updated after reclamation.
Measure reclaimed rights against the license position or optimization target.
validationChecklist: Only reclaimable software is targeted.
Removal is confirmed after action.
Compliance position or cost optimization improves measurably.
commonMistakes: Reclaiming without business-owner validation.
Counting expected savings before install evidence is updated.

title: Implement an unauthorized-software exception process
question: How would you handle unauthorized software that cannot be removed immediately?
summary: Combines SAM governance with risk acceptance.
scenario: An application is detected without approved entitlement, but it supports a critical legacy process and cannot be removed immediately.
exactAnswer: Treat it as a governed exception: document business need, risk, owner, compensating controls, and expiry; then pair the exception with a remediation plan such as procurement, replacement, or decommissioning.
navigationPath: All > GRC or policy exception workflow
steps: Validate the unauthorized software finding and identify business owner and dependency.
Create an exception record with justification, compensating controls, and expiry.
Create linked remediation tasks for procurement, migration, or removal.
Review the exception periodically until it is closed or converted to a different approved state.
validationChecklist: Exception has expiry and owner.
Compensating controls are defined.
Long-term remediation is tracked separately.
commonMistakes: Leaving unauthorized software in permanent exception state.
Creating an exception without a remediation path.

title: Implement blackout and maintenance-window enforcement
question: How would you prevent risky changes from being scheduled in blackout periods?
summary: Brings together calendars, conflict logic, and governance.
scenario: The organization wants changes blocked during known blackout windows unless an emergency override is approved.
exactAnswer: Store blackout and maintenance windows in a governed schedule source, validate requested change windows against those schedules during creation and updates, and provide a controlled emergency override path with explicit approval and auditability.
navigationPath: All > Business Rules or Flow Designer
steps: Define blackout and allowed-maintenance schedules by service, environment, or region as needed.
Validate requested change windows against those schedules during create and update.
Block ordinary changes when the selected window violates policy.
Provide an override path for emergency changes with explicit approval and audit trail.
Report on attempted blackout violations and emergency overrides.
validationChecklist: Normal changes are blocked during blackout windows.
Emergency overrides are auditable.
Service-specific windows are handled correctly.
commonMistakes: Using one generic blackout calendar for all services regardless of reality.
Allowing silent overrides without governance.

title: Design a troubleshooting flow for failing discovery patterns
question: How would you troubleshoot a pattern-based discovery that identifies the host but misses application details?
scenario: A discovery run creates the server CI but fails to populate the application instances the team expects.
exactAnswer: Troubleshoot in order: confirm the pattern is applicable to the target technology, review credentials and commands, inspect pattern execution steps and logs, validate identification sections, and compare discovered evidence against what the pattern expects.
steps: Confirm the target host matches the technology and version supported by the pattern.
Review credentials, access method, and any command or API failures in logs.
Inspect pattern execution to see which section stops returning expected data.
Validate the identification and relation steps for the application records.
Retest with a narrow target and compare actual evidence to pattern expectations.
validationChecklist: Host access is successful.
Pattern steps return expected evidence.
Application CIs are created with the correct relationships.
commonMistakes: Assuming the pattern supports the target technology version without checking.
Troubleshooting only the final CI output and not the step-level execution evidence.
